passages.explanation: As the church grows, a practical problem emerges: Greek-speaking Jewish widows are being overlooked in the daily food distribution while Hebrew-speaking widows are taken care of. This is the first internal conflict in the church — and it falls along ethnic and cultural lines. The twelve apostles call the whole community together and say it wouldn't be right for them to neglect preaching to manage food distribution. They propose choosing seven men to handle this responsibility so the apostles can focus on prayer and the word.

The community agrees and selects seven men, all with Greek names — suggesting they're from the very group that felt neglected. This is a wise move: the people affected by the problem are given authority to solve it. The seven include Stephen, described as full of faith and the Holy Spirit, and Nicolas, a convert from Antioch. The apostles pray over them and lay hands on them. The word of God keeps spreading, the number of disciples grows rapidly in Jerusalem, and even a large number of priests become believers.

Stephen, full of grace and power, performs great signs and wonders among the people. Members of a synagogue of freed slaves (from Cyrene, Alexandria, Cilicia, and Asia) begin arguing with Stephen, but they can't stand up against his wisdom or the Spirit by which he speaks. Stephen was appointed to serve tables, but God has bigger plans for him.

Unable to win the argument fairly, they secretly get men to make false accusations: they claim Stephen blasphemes Moses and God. They stir up the people, the elders, and the scribes, who seize Stephen and bring him before the council. False witnesses testify that Stephen never stops speaking against the temple and the law, and that Jesus of Nazareth will destroy the temple and change the customs Moses handed down. As everyone in the council stares at Stephen, his face looks like the face of an angel.
